facts.text: Paris is known as the City of Light because it was one of the first European cities to adopt widespread street lighting in the 1860s.
Tokyo's Shibuya Crossing is the busiest pedestrian intersection in the world, with up to 3,000 people crossing at once during peak times.
New York City's subway system has 472 stations, making it the largest rapid transit system in the world by number of stations.
The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and was used for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to stand for only 20 years after the 1889 World's Fair before being dismantled.
Istanbul is the only city in the world that spans two continents — Europe and Asia — divided by the Bosphorus Strait.
Singapore is both a city and a country, making it one of only three surviving city-states in the world.
Rio de Janeiro's Christ the Redeemer statue stands 98 feet tall atop Mount Corcovado at 2,300 feet above sea level.
London's Big Ben is actually the name of the bell inside the Elizabeth Tower, not the tower itself.
The Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco is painted a color called International Orange, chosen for visibility in fog.
Bangkok's full ceremonial name in Thai is 168 letters long, making it the longest city name in the world.
Sydney Opera House took 16 years to build, from 1957 to 1973, and cost 14 times its original budget estimate.
New Orleans is famous for its jazz music scene, which originated in the early 20th century in neighborhoods like Tremé and Storyville.
Amsterdam has more bicycles than people — approximately 881,000 bikes for about 872,000 residents.
The Burj Khalifa in Dubai stands at 2,717 feet, making it the tallest building in the world since its completion in 2010.
Rome is called the Eternal City because ancient Romans believed the city would endure forever, no matter what happened to the empire.
According to legend, Rome was founded by Romulus on April 21, 753 BC, a date still celebrated as Natale di Roma.
Prague's Old Town Square has an astronomical clock installed in 1410, making it the oldest still-operating astronomical clock in the world.
Tokyo has more Michelin-starred restaurants than any other city in the world, with over 200 starred establishments.
The London Underground, opened in 1863, was the world's first underground railway system.
Cape Town is home to Table Mountain, one of the oldest mountains on Earth at roughly 600 million years old.
Chicago earned the nickname The Windy City, though it likely refers to its boastful politicians rather than the weather.
Venice's gondolas have been asymmetrically built since the 19th century so a single gondolier can row them in a straight line.
Mexico City was built on the ruins of Tenochtitlán, the capital of the Aztec Empire, which sat on an island in Lake Texcoco.
The Statue of Liberty was a gift from France to the United States and was dedicated on October 28, 1886.
Seoul's Gangnam District became world-famous after the 2012 hit song Gangnam Style by PSY.
Hong Kong has more skyscrapers than any other city in the world, with over 480 buildings exceeding 150 meters.
Buenos Aires has the widest avenue in the world — Avenida 9 de Julio is 140 meters wide with up to 16 lanes of traffic.
Nashville is known as Music City, a nickname it earned due to its central role in the country music industry.
Barcelona's La Sagrada Familia basilica has been under construction since 1882 and is not expected to be completed until 2026.
Marrakech's Jemaa el-Fnaa square transforms each evening into a massive open-air food market with hundreds of stalls.
Dubai's Palm Jumeirah is an artificial archipelago that added 78 kilometers of coastline to the city.
Vienna was the home of legendary composers Mozart, Beethoven, Haydn, Schubert, Strauss, and Brahms.
The New York City Marathon is the largest marathon in the world, with over 50,000 finishers annually.
Lisbon is one of the oldest cities in Western Europe, predating London, Paris, and Rome by centuries.
San Francisco's cable cars are the only mobile National Historic Landmark in the United States.
Cairo is home to the Great Pyramid of Giza, the only one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World still standing.
Toronto's PATH system is the largest underground shopping complex in the world, stretching over 30 kilometers.
Las Vegas's famous Strip is actually not within the city limits of Las Vegas — it is in the unincorporated town of Paradise.
Athens is considered the birthplace of democracy, with the first democratic system established around 508 BC.
Mumbai's Dabbawalas deliver over 200,000 home-cooked lunches daily with an error rate of roughly one in six million deliveries.
The Paris Metro was opened on July 19, 1900, to serve visitors to the Paris World's Fair.
Berlin's East Side Gallery is the longest remaining section of the Berlin Wall, stretching 1.3 kilometers and covered in murals.
Los Angeles was originally named El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles del Río Porciúncula by Spanish settlers.
Kyoto was the imperial capital of Japan for over 1,000 years, from 794 to 1868.
The London Eye was the world's tallest Ferris wheel when it opened in 2000, standing 135 meters high.
Chicago is home to the first skyscraper ever built — the Home Insurance Building, completed in 1885.
Bali is known as the Island of the Gods and has over 10,000 Hindu temples across the island.
Washington DC was designed by French architect Pierre Charles L'Enfant, who planned the city's grid with wide avenues and circles.
Shanghai's Maglev train is the fastest commercial train in the world, reaching speeds of 431 km/h (268 mph).
Boston was the site of the Boston Tea Party in 1773, a pivotal event leading to the American Revolution.
Rome has a law that says cats are free to roam the city and cannot be evicted from any public ruin.
Miami's Art Deco Historic District in South Beach contains the largest collection of Art Deco architecture in the world.
Paris has an entire city beneath it — the Catacombs hold the remains of more than six million people in underground tunnels.
Singapore's Changi Airport regularly tops lists of the best airports in the world, featuring a butterfly garden, rooftop pool, and indoor waterfall.
New York City's Central Park is larger than the country of Monaco, covering 843 acres.
Tokyo's Tsukiji Outer Market remains one of the world's most famous food destinations despite the inner wholesale market relocating to Toyosu in 2018.
The London Underground map designed by Harry Beck in 1931 became the template for metro maps used by transit systems worldwide.
Istanbul's Grand Bazaar is one of the oldest and largest covered markets in the world, with over 4,000 shops.
Barcelona's La Boqueria market on La Rambla has been a food market since the early 1200s.
Dubai went from a small fishing village to one of the wealthiest cities in the world in less than 50 years.
Amsterdam's canal ring was built during the Dutch Golden Age in the 17th century and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
New Orleans celebrates Mardi Gras with massive parades and festivities that can attract over one million visitors.
Prague is nicknamed the City of a Hundred Spires due to its many Gothic, Baroque, and Renaissance church towers.
São Paulo has the largest Japanese population outside Japan, with over 1.5 million people of Japanese descent.
The Sydney Harbour Bridge is nicknamed The Coathanger due to its arch-based design.
Vienna's Sachertorte, a rich chocolate cake, was invented in 1832 by 16-year-old apprentice Franz Sacher.
Athens' Parthenon has stood atop the Acropolis since 438 BC and remains one of the most iconic structures in Western civilization.
San Francisco's Chinatown, established in 1848, is the oldest Chinatown in North America.
Mexico City is sinking at a rate of up to 50 centimeters per year because it was built on a drained lakebed.
Seoul has the fastest average internet speed of any major city in the world.
The Great Wall of China's nearest sections to Beijing at Badaling and Mutianyu draw millions of visitors each year.
Rio de Janeiro's Carnival is the world's largest carnival celebration, attracting about two million people per day.
Lisbon's Tram 28 is one of the most famous tram routes in the world, winding through the city's oldest neighborhoods.
Chicago deep-dish pizza originated at Pizzeria Uno in 1943, created by Ike Sewell and Ric Riccardo.
Hong Kong's Star Ferry has been shuttling passengers across Victoria Harbour since 1888.
Berlin's Alexanderplatz TV Tower is the tallest structure in Germany at 368 meters.
Nashville's Grand Ole Opry has been broadcasting live country music since 1925, making it the longest-running radio show in US history.
Tokyo's train system is so punctual that if a train is more than five minutes late, passengers receive a delay certificate for their employers.
Cape Town's Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden is one of the great botanical gardens of the world, established in 1913.
Marrakech's medina, the old walled city, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site filled with narrow winding alleys and traditional souks.
Paris has 37 bridges crossing the Seine River, the oldest being the Pont Neuf, completed in 1607.
London has hosted the Summer Olympic Games three times — in 1908, 1948, and 2012.
Toronto's CN Tower held the record as the world's tallest free-standing structure for 32 years, from 1975 to 2007.
Buenos Aires has more bookstores per capita than any other city in the world.
The Empire State Building in New York was built in just 410 days, finishing in April 1931.
Dubai's indoor ski slope at the Mall of the Emirates maintains real snow in the middle of the desert.
Los Angeles has over 100 museums, more than any other city in the United States.
Mumbai is home to Bollywood, the largest film industry in the world by number of films produced annually.
Rome's Trevi Fountain collects roughly 3,000 euros in coins each day, which is donated to charity.
Bangkok is the most visited city in the world by international tourists, surpassing London and Paris.
Amsterdam's Rijksmuseum houses Rembrandt's Night Watch and over 8,000 other works of art and historical objects.
Washington DC's Smithsonian Institution is the world's largest museum, education, and research complex with 21 museums — all free.
Istanbul was previously known as Constantinople and before that as Byzantium, having been renamed multiple times through history.
Kyoto has 17 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, more than any other city in Japan.
Boston's Freedom Trail is a 2.5-mile walking path through 16 historically significant sites from the American Revolution.
Singapore banned chewing gum in 1992, and it remains illegal to sell or import it without a prescription.
Las Vegas uses about 15 percent of all the neon in the world for its famous signs and displays.
The Great Sphinx of Giza near Cairo is one of the oldest and largest sculptures in the world, believed to have been built around 2500 BC.
Milan's fashion district, the Quadrilatero della Moda, is one of the most important fashion centers in the world.
New York City's Times Square attracts approximately 50 million visitors per year, making it one of the world's most visited tourist attractions.
Shanghai's skyline in the Pudong district was mostly farmland as recently as 1990.
The Leaning Tower of Pisa took 199 years to build and began leaning during construction when the soil beneath it shifted.
Copenhagen has been ranked the most bike-friendly city in the world, with more bikes than cars.
Lima, Peru is considered one of the top food capitals of the world, known for ceviche and a thriving culinary scene.
San Francisco's Golden Gate Bridge took four years to build and opened on May 27, 1937.
Paris's Louvre Museum is the most visited art museum in the world, with nearly 10 million visitors annually.
Prague's Charles Bridge was completed in 1402 and features 30 Baroque statues along its length.
Helsinki has been recognized as a UNESCO City of Design, reflecting Finland's world-renowned design culture.
Havana, Cuba is famous for its colorful vintage American cars from the 1950s that still serve as everyday transportation.
Tokyo Disneyland, opened in 1983, was the first Disney theme park built outside the United States.
London's Harrods department store covers five acres and has over 330 departments, making it one of the largest stores in the world.
Athens hosted the first modern Olympic Games in 1896.
Chicago's elevated train system, known as the L, forms a loop in the downtown area that gave the Loop neighborhood its name.
Stockholm is built on 14 islands connected by 57 bridges, earning it the nickname Venice of the North.
Cairo's Khan el-Khalili bazaar has been a major market since the 14th century and remains one of the Middle East's largest marketplaces.
Montreal hosts the world's largest jazz festival, the Festival International de Jazz de Montréal, every summer.
The Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur were the tallest buildings in the world from 1998 to 2004.
Venice has about 400 gondolas operating today, compared to over 10,000 in the 18th century.
Los Angeles' Hollywood sign was originally erected in 1923 as Hollywoodland to advertise a real estate development.
Vienna's Spanish Riding School, founded in 1572, is the oldest riding school in the world that still practises classical dressage.
San Francisco's Alcatraz Island served as a federal prison from 1934 to 1963 and is now one of the city's top tourist attractions.
Dubai's Deira Gold Souk contains over 300 gold retailers and holds an estimated 10 tons of gold at any time.
Edinburgh hosts the world's largest annual arts festival, the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, every August.
New York City has approximately 26,000 restaurants, reflecting cuisines from every corner of the world.
Tokyo's Akihabara district is the global center of anime, manga, and electronics culture.
Barcelona's Park Güell, designed by Antoni Gaudí, features the world's longest bench covered in colorful mosaic tiles.
Munich's Oktoberfest is the world's largest beer festival, attracting over six million visitors annually.
New Orleans is the birthplace of jazz music, which emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
Beijing's Forbidden City is the world's largest palace complex, covering 178 acres with 980 buildings.
Amsterdam's Anne Frank House museum attracts over one million visitors per year.
Reykjavik is the northernmost capital city of a sovereign state in the world.
Chicago's Willis Tower (formerly Sears Tower) has a glass-bottomed Skydeck Ledge that extends 4.3 feet out from the 103rd floor.
Rio de Janeiro's Sugarloaf Mountain offers panoramic views of the city and Guanabara Bay from its 1,299-foot summit.
The Tokyo subway system carries more than 8 million passengers daily, one of the busiest in the world.
Bruges, Belgium is often called the Venice of the North due to its extensive canal system and medieval architecture.
Paris has over 470 parks and gardens, including the famous Luxembourg Gardens and Tuileries Garden.
Istanbul's Hagia Sophia has served as a cathedral, a mosque, a museum, and then a mosque again over its 1,500-year history.
London's Buckingham Palace has 775 rooms, including 78 bathrooms and 19 state rooms.
Cusco, Peru was the historic capital of the Inca Empire and sits at over 11,000 feet above sea level.
Nashville's hot chicken, a spicy fried chicken dish, originated at Prince's Hot Chicken Shack in the 1930s.
Sydney's Bondi Beach is one of the most famous beaches in the world and has been a public beach since 1882.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic and Pacific oceans and took ten years to build, opening on August 15, 1914.
Lisbon's Belém Tower was built in 1515 as a ceremonial gateway to the city and a fort defending the Tagus River.
Seoul's Myeongdong district is one of Asia's largest shopping districts, drawing over two million visitors daily.
Cape Town's Victoria & Alfred Waterfront is the most visited destination in South Africa, attracting 24 million visitors annually.
Rome's Pantheon has the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, built nearly 2,000 years ago and still standing.
Bogotá, Colombia closes 75 miles of roads every Sunday for Ciclovía, allowing cyclists and pedestrians to take over the streets.
New York's Brooklyn Bridge, opened in 1883, was the first steel-wire suspension bridge ever constructed.
Madrid's Museo del Prado houses one of the finest collections of European art, with over 8,000 paintings.
Singapore's Gardens by the Bay features Supertrees — vertical gardens up to 50 meters tall that function as environmental engines.
Tokyo's Shinjuku Station is the busiest train station in the world, serving over 3.5 million passengers daily.
Florence is known as the birthplace of the Renaissance and is home to Michelangelo's David sculpture.
Buenos Aires' La Boca neighborhood is famous for its brightly painted buildings and tango street performers.
London's West End has been a major theater district since the 17th century and rivals Broadway for live stage performances.
Miami's Little Havana neighborhood is the cultural heart of Cuban-American life and is named after Cuba's capital city.
Berlin's Brandenburg Gate was trapped in no-man's land during the Cold War and became a symbol of German reunification.
Bangkok's Grand Palace complex covers over 200,000 square meters and has been the official residence of Thai kings since 1782.
The Vatican City, located within Rome, is the smallest independent state in the world at just 0.17 square miles.
San Francisco's Fisherman's Wharf has been a working waterfront since the Gold Rush era of the 1850s.
Copenhagen's Tivoli Gardens, opened in 1843, is one of the oldest amusement parks in the world and inspired Walt Disney.
Cairo sits on the banks of the Nile River and is the largest city in the Arab world and Africa.
Paris's Arc de Triomphe sits at the center of 12 radiating avenues and honors those who fought for France.
Osaka is considered the food capital of Japan, with locals following the expression kuidaore, meaning to eat until you drop.
Toronto's annual International Film Festival is one of the most prestigious in the world and has launched dozens of Oscar-winning films.
London's Tower of London has served as a royal palace, prison, armory, treasury, and zoo over its nearly 1,000-year history.
The Hollywood Walk of Fame in Los Angeles has more than 2,700 stars embedded in the sidewalk along Hollywood Boulevard.
Marrakech's Majorelle Garden was restored by fashion designer Yves Saint Laurent and features vivid blue buildings.
Hong Kong's Victoria Peak offers stunning panoramic views of the city skyline and Victoria Harbour.
Washington DC's National Mall stretches two miles from the Capitol Building to the Lincoln Memorial.
Venice holds an annual Carnival celebration featuring elaborate masks and costumes dating back to the 12th century.
The Great Fire of London in 1666 destroyed over 13,000 houses and 87 churches but led to the city being rebuilt in stone and brick.
Lima's historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site featuring colonial architecture from the 16th century.
Seoul's Bukchon Hanok Village preserves hundreds of traditional Korean houses dating back to the Joseon dynasty.
New York's Grand Central Terminal is one of the most visited tourist attractions in the world, with approximately 750,000 visitors daily.
Vienna's Naschmarkt has been a food market since the 16th century and offers everything from Viennese pastries to international cuisine.
Istanbul has over 3,000 mosques, the most of any city in the world.
Mexico City's Zócalo is one of the largest public squares in the world and has been the city's main gathering place since Aztec times.
Amsterdam has over 100 kilometers of canals, about 90 islands, and 1,500 bridges.
Dubrovnik's old city walls stretch for nearly two kilometers and were used as a filming location for King's Landing in Game of Thrones.
Chicago's Millennium Park features Cloud Gate, commonly called The Bean, a 110-ton polished stainless steel sculpture.
Bangkok has over 400 Buddhist temples, known as wats, including the famous Wat Phra Kaew and Wat Arun.
San Francisco experienced a devastating earthquake and fire on April 18, 1906, destroying over 80 percent of the city.
London's Notting Hill Carnival is the largest street festival in Europe, celebrating Caribbean culture every August.
Dubai's Palm Jumeirah can be seen from space and was built using enough sand to fill 2.5 Empire State Buildings.
Tokyo's Meiji Shrine is surrounded by a forest of 120,000 trees donated from all over Japan when it was built in 1920.
The oldest continuously inhabited city in the world is Damascus, Syria, with evidence of habitation dating back 11,000 years.
Rome's Trevi Fountain was featured in the 1960 film La Dolce Vita and has been a symbol of romance in cinema ever since.
Nairobi is one of the few major cities in the world with a national park within its borders — Nairobi National Park.
Paris's Moulin Rouge cabaret has been entertaining audiences since it opened in 1889.
New York City's Statue of Liberty was assembled from 300 copper sheets and has a 35-foot waistline.
Prague's Old Town Square features an astronomical clock that puts on a show of moving apostle figures every hour.
Barcelona has nine UNESCO World Heritage Sites designed by architect Antoni Gaudí.
Las Vegas casinos have no clocks or windows to help visitors lose track of time while gambling.
Sydney hosted the 2000 Summer Olympics, widely regarded as one of the most successful Games in modern history.
Cape Town is one of the most biodiverse cities on Earth, located within the Cape Floristic Region with over 9,000 plant species.
Tokyo's Ginza district is one of the most expensive shopping areas in the world, with land prices rivaling Manhattan.
The Acropolis of Athens has been damaged by war, earthquakes, and pollution, yet has endured for nearly 2,500 years.
New Orleans' French Quarter is the oldest neighborhood in the city, founded by the French in 1718.
Berlin has more museums than rainy days per year, with over 170 museums throughout the city.
Mumbai's Marine Drive, a 3.6-kilometer arc-shaped boulevard, is nicknamed the Queen's Necklace for its nighttime lights.
Milan's Cathedral, the Duomo, took nearly six centuries to complete and features over 3,400 statues.
Havana's Malecón seawall stretches for 8 kilometers and is the social gathering spot for the city's residents.
London's Heathrow Airport is one of the busiest airports in the world, handling over 80 million passengers annually.
Petra, near Amman, Jordan, features buildings carved directly into rose-red cliff faces over 2,000 years ago.
Mexico City has the largest number of museums in the Americas, with over 150 recognized museums.
Amsterdam's Van Gogh Museum holds the largest collection of Van Gogh's works, with over 200 paintings and 500 drawings.
Buenos Aires is known as the Paris of South America due to its European-style architecture and café culture.
Shanghai's The Bund waterfront features 52 buildings in various architectural styles including Gothic, Baroque, and Art Deco.
Boston's Fenway Park, opened in 1912, is the oldest active Major League Baseball park in the United States.
Bangkok's floating markets, including Damnoen Saduak, are iconic images of Thai culture where vendors sell food from boats.
Rome is home to the smallest country in the world — Vatican City — which has its own postal service, radio station, and army.
Wellington, New Zealand is the world's southernmost capital city.
Paris's Shakespeare and Company bookshop has been a literary landmark since the 1920s, hosting writers like Hemingway and Joyce.
New York's Empire State Building was the tallest building in the world for nearly 40 years, from 1931 to 1970.
Lyon, France is considered the gastronomic capital of the world, known for its bouchons and Michelin-starred restaurants.
Istanbul's Basilica Cistern is an underground water reservoir built in 532 AD that holds 80,000 cubic meters of water.
Kyoto's Fushimi Inari Shrine features thousands of vermilion torii gates winding up Mount Inari.
San Francisco's Lombard Street is famous for its steep, one-block section featuring eight sharp hairpin turns.
Rio de Janeiro means River of January in Portuguese, named by explorers who arrived in January 1502.
Toronto is one of the most multicultural cities in the world — over 200 ethnic groups speak more than 140 languages.
London's Globe Theatre is a reconstruction of the original theater where Shakespeare's plays were first performed.
Chicago's Willis Tower has 16,100 windows that are washed eight times a year by a crew of window washers.
Dubai's Dubai Mall is one of the largest shopping malls in the world, with over 1,200 retail stores and an indoor aquarium.
Lisbon's hills give the city the nickname City of Seven Hills, much like Rome.
Singapore's hawker centers are open-air food courts that serve some of the best and cheapest street food in the world.
Athens was the first European Capital of Culture in 1985, a designation by the European Union.
New York City's Broadway theater district has about 41 professional theaters in a small area around Times Square.
Cape Town's Robben Island, where Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 18 years, is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and museum.
Oslo, Norway's capital, is surrounded by forests and fjords and is one of the greenest cities in Europe.
Chicago hosted the World's Columbian Exposition in 1893, which introduced the Ferris wheel, Cracker Jack, and Juicy Fruit gum.
Tokyo's cherry blossom season in spring draws millions of visitors to parks across the city for hanami flower-viewing parties.
Medellín, Colombia transformed from one of the most dangerous cities in the world to an innovation hub through urban planning.
Paris's Notre-Dame Cathedral, started in 1163, is one of the finest examples of French Gothic architecture.
London's Brick Lane is famous for its vibrant street art, curry houses, and vintage markets.
Miami's South Beach is known for its pastel-colored Art Deco buildings dating from the 1930s and 1940s.
Marrakech is called the Red City due to the distinctive reddish-pink color of its walls and buildings.
The Sagrada Familia in Barcelona receives over 4.5 million visitors per year, making it Spain's most visited monument.
Seoul's Lotte World Tower stands at 555 meters and is the fifth tallest building in the world.
New York City's subway runs 24 hours a day, 7 days a week — one of the few transit systems in the world to do so.
Ho Chi Minh City, formerly Saigon, is Vietnam's largest city and economic hub with a population of over nine million.
Venice is built on 118 small islands separated by canals and has no roads — transportation is entirely by boat or on foot.
Hong Kong's Kowloon Walled City was once the most densely populated place on Earth with 33,000 people in 6.4 acres before its demolition in 1993.
Berlin's Museum Island in the Spree River contains five world-renowned museums and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Los Angeles is the entertainment capital of the world, home to major film studios including Warner Bros, Universal, and Paramount.
Istanbul connects Europe and Asia via two suspension bridges and a rail tunnel crossing the Bosphorus.
Nashville has more than 180 live music venues, earning it the reputation of having live music on every corner.
Denver, Colorado is called the Mile High City because its elevation is exactly one mile (5,280 feet) above sea level.
Barcelona's Las Ramblas pedestrian boulevard stretches 1.2 kilometers from Plaça de Catalunya to the waterfront.
Beijing's Temple of Heaven complex is four times the size of the Forbidden City.
The population of Tokyo's greater metropolitan area exceeds 37 million, making it the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
Amsterdam was founded as a small fishing village at the mouth of the Amstel River in the late 12th century.
Cairo's City of the Dead is a vast necropolis where some families have taken up residence among the tombs.
New York's One World Trade Center opened on November 3, 2014, standing 1,776 feet tall — a symbolic reference to American independence.
London's Borough Market has been trading in some form since the 13th century, making it one of the oldest food markets in the world.
Prague's beer consumption per capita is the highest in the world, with Czech citizens drinking about 140 liters per person annually.
Singapore's Marina Bay Sands hotel features a rooftop infinity pool 57 stories above ground, stretching 150 meters.
Buenos Aires has the widest avenue and the longest avenue in the world — Avenida 9 de Julio and Avenida Rivadavia respectively.
Rome has a drinking fountain called a nasone on nearly every major street, providing free clean water to residents and visitors.
Dubai Frame is a 150-meter-tall picture-frame-shaped building that frames views of old and new Dubai from each side.
Hanoi is one of the oldest capital cities in the world, celebrating its 1,000th anniversary in 2010.
Los Angeles has approximately 300 sunny days per year, one of the sunniest cities in the United States.
Kraków's Main Square, Rynek Główny, is the largest medieval town square in Europe at about 40,000 square meters.
Bangkok's Chatuchak Weekend Market has over 15,000 stalls and is one of the largest outdoor markets in the world.
Washington DC's cherry blossoms were a gift of 3,000 trees from the Mayor of Tokyo in 1912.
The Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur are connected by a sky bridge at the 41st floor, 170 meters above the ground.
Helsinki's unique architectural blend includes Art Nouveau, neoclassical, and modern styles, including Alvar Aalto's designs.
The ancient city of Varanasi in India is one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, dating back over 3,000 years.
London's Covent Garden was originally a fruit and vegetable market before becoming a shopping and entertainment district.
Tokyo's Harajuku district is the epicenter of Japanese youth culture and street fashion.
Chicago's Navy Pier extends 3,300 feet into Lake Michigan and is one of the most visited attractions in the Midwest.
Jaipur is known as the Pink City because in 1876 the entire old city was painted terracotta pink to welcome the Prince of Wales.
San Francisco's fog is so famous that locals have given it the name Karl.
Cartagena, Colombia's walled old city is a UNESCO World Heritage Site featuring colorful colonial architecture.
Paris's Père Lachaise Cemetery is the most visited cemetery in the world, where Jim Morrison, Oscar Wilde, and Édith Piaf are buried.
New York City produces enough trash each day to fill the Empire State Building.
Istanbul's Topkapi Palace served as the primary residence of Ottoman sultans for almost 400 years.
Zurich consistently ranks as one of the most expensive cities in the world but also one of the highest in quality of life.
Seoul's Dongdaemun Design Plaza, designed by Zaha Hadid, is a major urban landmark built on a historic site.
Las Vegas's Bellagio fountain show features over 1,000 fountains that shoot water up to 460 feet in the air choreographed to music.
Cape Town's Bo-Kaap neighborhood is famous for its brightly painted houses in the historic Malay Quarter.
Barcelona hosted the 1992 Summer Olympics, which transformed the city's waterfront and infrastructure.
London taxi drivers must pass The Knowledge, a grueling test requiring memorization of 25,000 streets within a six-mile radius.
Dubrovnik's city walls have never been breached by a hostile army throughout their centuries-long history.
Bangkok's Khao San Road is one of the most famous backpacker streets in the world.
Rome has over 900 churches, more than any other city in the world.
New York City's pizza culture is so strong that there are more than 1,600 pizzerias across the five boroughs.
Angkor Wat near Siem Reap, Cambodia is the largest religious monument in the world, spanning over 400 acres.
Amsterdam's Red Light District, De Wallen, dates back to the 14th century when the city was a major port.
Helsinki's Temppeliaukio Church is carved directly into solid rock and is one of Finland's most visited attractions.
Dubai's artificial islands include The World, a cluster of 300 islands shaped like a world map visible from space.
Mexico City's Palacio de Bellas Artes is considered the most important cultural center in the country with murals by Diego Rivera.
The Berlin Wall divided Berlin for 28 years, from 1961 to 1989.
Paris has only one stop sign in the entire city, located at the exit of a building company's yard near the Quai Saint-Exupéry.
Tokyo's Tsukiji Outer Market is known for serving the freshest sushi in the world.
Buenos Aires developed a unique tango culture that UNESCO recognized as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2009.
New Orleans' above-ground cemeteries are called Cities of the Dead because the high water table prevents traditional burials.
Sydney's New Year's Eve fireworks display over the Harbour Bridge is one of the most-watched celebrations in the world.
Hong Kong's MTR system has an on-time rate of over 99.9 percent, making it one of the most reliable transit systems globally.
Edinburgh Castle sits atop Castle Rock, an extinct volcanic plug that has been a fortified site for over 3,000 years.
Mumbai's Gateway of India was built to commemorate the visit of King George V and Queen Mary in 1911.
Athens has continuous habitation for over 5,000 years, making it one of the oldest cities in the world.
Boston's Big Dig, completed in 2007, was the most expensive highway project in US history at over $14.6 billion.
Vienna's Theater an der Wien premiered Beethoven's Fidelio in 1805 and remains one of the world's great opera houses.
The Taj Mahal, near Agra, India, was built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife and took 22 years to complete.
Shanghai's Yu Garden has been a tranquil retreat in the city since 1559 during the Ming Dynasty.
London's Natural History Museum houses over 80 million specimens from the natural world, some dating back billions of years.
San Francisco's cable car system is the last manually operated cable car system in the world.
Prague is the fourteenth-largest city in the European Union and is sometimes called the Heart of Europe.
Bangkok residents spend an average of 72 minutes commuting each day, among the longest commute times in the world.
Chicago's Field Museum houses Sue, the largest and most complete Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton ever discovered.
Rio de Janeiro's Ipanema Beach inspired the famous bossa nova song The Girl from Ipanema in 1962.
Singapore's strict laws include heavy fines for jaywalking, littering, and failing to flush a public toilet.
Paris is the most visited city in Europe, with over 30 million international tourists arriving annually.
Istanbul's Blue Mosque gets its nickname from the 20,000 handmade blue Iznik tiles decorating its interior.
New York City has 520 miles of subway track, enough to stretch from Manhattan to Toronto.
Lisbon was nearly destroyed by a massive earthquake, tsunami, and fire on November 1, 1755, one of the deadliest natural disasters in European history.
Los Angeles' Griffith Observatory has been featured in numerous films and offers free admission to the public.
Amsterdam's Concert­gebouw is regarded as having some of the finest acoustics of any concert hall in the world.
Tokyo hosted the 1964 Summer Olympics, which showcased Japan's postwar recovery and introduced the bullet train.
Cairo's Egyptian Museum holds over 120,000 artifacts including the golden mask of Tutankhamun.
Seville's Alcázar palace is the oldest royal palace still in use in Europe, originally built in the 10th century.
Boston's Harvard University, founded in 1636, is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States.
Dubai's metro system is the longest driverless, fully automated metro network in the world.
Rome's SPQR initials stand for Senatus Populusque Romanus and can still be seen on manhole covers and buildings throughout the city.
Montreal's underground city, RÉSO, is the largest underground complex in the world with 32 kilometers of tunnels.
Seoul was named the World Design Capital in 2010, recognized for using design to improve urban life.
Kyoto's Kinkaku-ji, the Golden Pavilion, is covered in actual gold leaf and reflects beautifully on the surrounding pond.
New York's Wall Street gets its name from a literal wall that Dutch colonists built in the 17th century to protect against attacks.
Barcelona's Magic Fountain of Montjuïc puts on free light and music shows that attract over 2.5 million spectators per year.
London's Oyster card transit system is named after the phrase the world is your oyster.
Ho Chi Minh City has more motorbikes than people, with over 7.5 million registered motorcycles for a population of about 9 million.
The Sydney Opera House's roof is made of over one million tiles, all manufactured in Sweden.
Addis Ababa, Ethiopia is the highest capital city in Africa, sitting at about 2,355 meters above sea level.
Chicago's Wrigley Field has had ivy growing on its outfield walls since 1937.
Vienna consistently tops global quality-of-life and livability rankings.
Paris's Champs-Élysées is often considered the most beautiful avenue in the world, stretching 1.9 kilometers.
New York's Metropolitan Museum of Art has a collection of over two million works spanning 5,000 years of history.
Singapore's total land area is only about 733 square kilometers but it continues to grow through land reclamation.
Tokyo's Robot Restaurant in Shinjuku features colorfully lit performances with robots, dancers, and lasers.
Cairo's Al-Azhar University, founded in 970 AD, is one of the oldest universities in the world.
Las Vegas gets its name from the Spanish word for the meadows, named by Spanish travelers who found water in the desert.
Prague's astronomical clock draws thousands of tourists every hour to watch its mechanical show of 12 apostle figures.
Istanbul's Bosphorus Bridge, opened in 1973, was the first bridge to connect Europe and Asia.
Portland, Oregon is known as the City of Roses and has been holding an annual Rose Festival since 1907.
London's Hyde Park covers 350 acres and hosts events including concerts, festivals, and the annual Winter Wonderland.
Buenos Aires' Café Tortoni, opened in 1858, is one of the oldest and most famous cafés in the world.
Rome has over 2,000 fountains, more than any other city in the world, including famous works by Bernini.
Nashville's full-scale replica of the Parthenon was originally built for the 1897 Tennessee Centennial Exposition.
Amsterdam is below sea level — about 26 percent of the Netherlands lies below sea level, protected by dikes and dunes.
Dubai hosts the world's richest horse race, the Dubai World Cup, with prize money exceeding $30 million.
Barcelona's La Rambla is actually five separate streets joined together to form one promenade.
Washington DC is not part of any US state — it is a federal district governed by Congress.
Seoul's subway system is one of the largest and most efficient in the world, with over 300 stations.
The Parthenon in Athens was originally painted in bright colors including red, blue, and gold — not the white marble seen today.
Tallinn, Estonia has one of the best-preserved medieval old towns in Europe, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Tokyo's vending machines number over 5 million across Japan, selling everything from hot coffee to fresh eggs.
Chicago's deep underground tunnels, built in the early 1900s for freight delivery, were largely forgotten until a flood in 1992.
Casablanca's Hassan II Mosque is the largest mosque in Africa and has the tallest minaret in the world at 210 meters.
Paris's Pont des Arts bridge once held over a million padlocks left by couples before they were removed in 2015 for safety.
New York City's High Line park was built on an abandoned elevated railway on Manhattan's West Side.
Budapest is actually two cities — Buda and Pest — that were unified in 1873 across the Danube River.
Manila, the capital of the Philippines, is the most densely populated city in the world.
Edinburgh has been a UNESCO City of Literature since 2004, the first city to receive the designation.
Los Angeles has the largest system of freeways in the world, totaling over 1,000 miles of highway.
Copenhagen's Nyhavn harbor with its colorful 17th-century townhouses was once home to Hans Christian Andersen.
St. Petersburg, Russia has the largest art museum in the world — the Hermitage — with over three million items in its collection.
Johannesburg is the world's largest city not situated on a river, lake, or coastline.
Tokyo's Imperial Palace sits on grounds that are worth more than all the real estate in California by some estimates.
San Francisco's Ghirardelli Square is the site of the original Ghirardelli chocolate factory, operating since 1852.
La Paz, Bolivia is the highest capital city in the world at roughly 3,640 meters above sea level.
Bangkok's Wat Pho temple contains the largest reclining Buddha statue in Thailand, stretching 46 meters long.
London's Soho district was originally a royal hunting ground — the name comes from a hunting cry.
Istanbul has been the capital of three empires — the Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman empires.
Seattle is nicknamed the Emerald City due to its lush evergreen forests and surrounding greenery.
Dubai's Miracle Garden is the world's largest natural flower garden with over 150 million flowers.
Bruges' historic belfry tower stands 83 meters tall and houses a carillon of 47 bells.
New York City has over 800 languages spoken within its borders, making it the most linguistically diverse city in the world.
Moscow's metro stations are nicknamed underground palaces for their ornate chandeliers, marble walls, and mosaic art.
Cape Town's Table Mountain is estimated to be one of the oldest mountains on Earth, predating the Andes, Rockies, and Himalayas.
Quito, Ecuador sits at 2,850 meters above sea level and was the first city designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978.
Philadelphia was the first capital of the United States and is where the Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776.
Venice's annual Regata Storica features a historical pageant of decorated boats followed by gondola races on the Grand Canal.
Toronto's Yonge Street was once listed in the Guinness Book of Records as the longest street in the world at 1,896 km.
Shanghai has the world's most extensive bus rapid transit system, carrying more than 2.5 million passengers per day.
